# Methods & measures {.unnumbered}

This appendix documents **how each measure in the book is defined and its caveats**,
so the chapters can stay narrative while interested readers can verify the
underlying choices. For dataset provenance (publisher, coverage, download lists),
see the [Sources](sources.qmd) appendix and the tracked `SOURCE.md` files under
`data/raw/`.

## Conventions used throughout

- **Year labels are school-year-ending.** "2025" means the **2024–25** school year.
  The one exception is the [budget-vote](../front/contingency-budgets.qmd) chapter,
  which indexes by the vote's *calendar* year (the May 2024 vote is "2024"); the
  budget adopted there funds the *following* school year.
- **Enrollment is the BEDS-day count** — students enrolled on the first Wednesday
  of October.
- **Comparison groups.** Charts use a 5-district graph group; tables use a
  12-district group (every Washington County K-12 district plus Schuylerville and
  Hoosick Falls). Putnam CSD is excluded because it is K-8 with no high school.
  See the [overview](../index.qmd).
- **Charter schools are excluded** — they have no district-total row in the source
  data, so all figures are district-operated.
- **Dollars are nominal** — not adjusted for inflation — unless noted. Part of any
  upward drift in money measures reflects rising prices, not rising real resources.
- **`nysed_district_cd`** (the 8-digit NYSED district code) is the stable join key
  across datasets; each source's native id maps to it through a tracked crosswalk.
  (The budget-vote files are the exception — they carry district *names*, not
  codes — so the vote chapter's Cambridge/neighbor surfacing is by name match.)

## Measures and their caveats

### Demographics (county population)

- **County population** is the July-1 resident total (Census Bureau basis),
  shown **indexed** to a base year (2000 = 100, or 2020 = 100) so the lines
  compare *trends* across counties of very different sizes, not raw counts.
- **New York** history and forecast come from the sibling *popfc* cohort-
  component project (baseline scenario); **Vermont** counties (Bennington,
  Rutland) come straight from the Census Bureau, which has no popfc forecast.
  These are the book's only **calendar-year** figures — the one place that
  departs from the school-year-ending convention.
- **Components of change** (births, deaths, migration) are shown for Washington
  County only. The **domestic vs international migration split is available only
  for history (through 2025)**; the forecast projects just total net migration,
  so the split columns are blank for forecast years. **2020 is omitted** from the
  components table because the 2020 Census boundary makes that year a partial
  April–July period in the source. Forecast lines (from 2025) are dashed.

### Enrollment and teachers

- **K-12 enrollment** — BEDS-day count (above).
- **Teacher counts** — a NYSED **headcount** (the number of people employed as
  teachers, all grades K-12), **not** full-time-equivalents. Teacher counts are
  available from **2017–18**, so any series that depends on them (such as students
  per teacher) begins at school-year-ending **2018**, even though enrollment runs
  back to 2015–16.
- **Students per teacher** = K-12 enrollment ÷ teacher headcount. It is an
  **approximation for cross-district and over-time comparison, not NYSED's official
  class-size figure** and not a count of children in a typical classroom.

### Class size

- **NYSED "average class size"** is **roster/section-based** — students in a
  section ÷ number of sections — reported only for a handful of specific grades and
  subjects, and **only since 2018–19**. Where the book summarizes it, it takes the
  **median across the reported classes** to resist small-section artifacts (a
  2-student physics class, a pandemic-year glitch).
- It is distinct from the headcount-based *students per teacher* (above) and the
  FTE-based NCES ratio (below).

### Student test results (grades 3–8 ELA & Math)

- **Proficiency rate** = (students at levels 3+4+5) ÷ (students tested). This
  reproduces NYSED's own reported proficiency rate exactly in the modern-layout
  years.
- **Comparability has limits.** New York reset its tests and passing standards
  twice, so rates compare only **within three eras** — 2015–17 (Common Core),
  2018–22 (redesign), 2023–25 (Next Generation) — **not across** them. A change
  across an era boundary reflects the new standard as much as any change in
  learning.
- **2020 is absent** (tests cancelled) and **2021 is anomalous** (only about half
  of students participated; not representative).

### Spending per pupil

- **Current operating spending per pupil** = (General + Special Aid + School Lunch
  funds) **minus** capital outlay, debt service, and interfund transfers, divided
  by K-12 enrollment. It captures salaries, benefits, classroom and BOCES services,
  food service, and federally funded programs.
- **What it excludes, and why:** capital projects and the debt that funds them
  arrive in large, irregular lumps (they would make spending per pupil volatile),
  and interfund transfers would otherwise be counted twice.
- This is close to the "current spending per pupil" measure the U.S. Census and
  NCES use. **Dollars are nominal.**

### Property taxes

- **Tax levy** — the total property tax the district collects.
- **Full-value tax rate** = levy ÷ taxable full value × 1,000 — the standard way
  to compare tax effort across places with different assessment practices.
- The rate can move sharply with a **reassessment** (which expands the tax base and
  lowers the rate even as the levy rises) — e.g., Washington County's reassessment
  around 2022–23.

### Property-tax cap

- A district may raise its levy by the **lesser of 2% or CPI** without special
  action; to exceed it the budget must win a **60% supermajority**.
- Each district's annual **levy limit** is set by the state formula; certain growth
  (pension contributions, capital projects, court-ordered payments) can be
  **excluded** from the cap.
- The over/under **gap is computed here as proposed levy − levy limit** (both
  excluding permissible exclusions). The source workbook's stored `levy_vs_limit`
  field is **not used** because NYSED flips its sign at year_end 2016.

### Reserves and fund balance

- **Reserve funds** are restricted accounts (A814–A884), each created under a
  specific statute for a specific purpose. **Fund balance** is classified
  restricted / committed / assigned (appropriated or not) / **unassigned**.
- The **4% cap** (tax-cap law) applies to the **unassigned** fund balance only —
  not to the restricted reserve funds. So a district can hold large restricted
  reserves and still be "at the cap."
- Per-reserve **legal restrictions** govern use: the capital reserve requires
  voter approval to establish **and** to expend; the tax-certiorari reserve is
  mandatory, capped at anticipated claims, with unused funds returned to the
  General Fund; the retirement reserve may be funded up to 2% of covered salaries
  per year (TRS sub-fund capped at 10%).

### Budget votes and contingency budgets

- A budget **passes** at **≥60% yes** if it is above the cap, else at a **simple
  majority (>50%)**. A district is **on contingency** if it failed the first vote
  and then either failed the re-vote or held none.
- **Cap status comes from NYSED's "60% required" flag** — the vote files carry no
  levy figures, only that flag.
- **Pre-2012 is omitted** (the tax cap took effect in 2012, so the above/below-cap
  split is undefined before then), and **2020 is atypical** (all-absentee,
  shifted re-vote date).

## Cross-checks and validation

- **NYSED headcount vs NCES FTE (students per teacher).** The book's NYSED
  headcount-based ratio and the independent NCES CCD full-time-equivalent ratio
  never differ by more than about two-thirds of a student per teacher, and both
  show the same sharp 2023–24 decline — so that turning point is not an artifact of
  how NYSED counts teachers.
- **Contingency defeats vs NYSED's statewide totals.** Computed first-vote defeat
  counts match NYSED's own published P/D statewide totals exactly for 2021–2025
  (within one for the anomalous 2020).
- **OSC ST-3 vs audited reports (reserves).** The as-filed OSC account-code
  reserve balances reconcile with Cambridge's audited annual financial reports
  (the ~$8,000 difference is a school-lunch encumbrance).
- **PTRC `% Yes` reconciliation.** Where NYSED's vote/levy workbooks include a
  "% Yes" column, the parsed Yes/No counts rebuild it to 0.0000 median error.

## Limitations — where comparisons break down

- **No inflation adjustment** in money measures — part of the upward drift is
  prices, not real growth.
- **Small districts are volatile.** Cambridge, Salem, and the other small peers
  have small counts in some cuts of the data (high-school sections, single test
  grades), so individual values can swing; the book prefers medians and notes this
  where it matters.
- **Per-pupil spending is mechanically higher for small districts** because fixed
  costs (a principal, a building, a bus route) spread over fewer students — a
  structural feature of scale, not inefficiency, and central to Cambridge's story.
- **Name-based matching** for budget votes (no codes) means a handful of re-votes
  each year don't link to a first-vote record in the older files; re-vote-outcome
  counts are therefore reliable from ~2020 on and approximate before.
- **Test-score eras and the tax cap** each create discrete breaks in their series
  (above) — trends must be read within an era, not across.
